basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-ʿĀdiyāt — “The Chargers” — opens with five impetuous oaths: mounts that gallop, snort, strike fire from the stones under their hooves, charge at dawn, raise dust, plunge into the heart of the throng. Five images of pure running — then the halt. And the verdict, without preamble: man is ungrateful to his Lord. Al-Qushayrī lingers on this word, kanūd (كَنُود), and gives three definitions of it that complete one another. The ungrateful, he says, is “the one who sees the trial that touches him, and does not see the favours that fill him.” He is also “the one whose head rests upon the cushion of grace, but whose heart wanders in the field of heedlessness (ghafla, غَفْلَة).” And he is simply “the one who forgets the favours and counts the misfortunes.” Three faces of one dry heart. Tustarī adds: the ungrateful is the one who has broken the Covenant (al-ʿahd, العَهْد), has parted from truthfulness (al-ṣidq, الصِّدْق), and has grown intimate with his passion (al-hawā, الهَوى) — and at that point God makes him despair of any uprightness or saving fear. The sūra, in eleven verses, says how such a dry heart forms, and where it leads.

versets.commentaire: God swears by beasts running until they are out of breath. al-ʿādiyāt (العَادِيَات), the ones who rush; ḍabḥ (ضَبْح), the rough breath of the gallop. The oath is an image before it is an argument: pure running, an élan that surpasses its strength, the very breath turned into sound. Some Sufis read these oaths as the figure of the ardent souls who rush toward God — and whose breath, at the end, is nothing but prayer. But the sūra does not pause at this beauty: it summons it precisely to contrast with the ingratitude about to be named. For man too knows how to run — toward what he loves.

mots.def: wa: oath particle.
ʿādiyāt: feminine plural active participle of ʿadā (to run, to rush); the ones who run — horses or camels in swift course.
ḍabḥ: the noisy, panting breath of galloping beasts; in the accusative as cognate object.

versets.commentaire: al-mūriyāt (المُورِيَات): those whose hooves wrest fire from the stone. Qadḥ (قَدْح), the stroke of striking flint. The course not only lights the night — it sets it ablaze. Qushayrī, who moves quickly past the literal tafsīr of these verses, suggests elsewhere that the true “sparks” are those the remembrance of God (dhikr, ذِكْر) draws from a hard heart. One spark is sometimes enough for a whole night to catch fire.

mots.def: coordinating particle: and then, next; links the successive oaths.
mūriyāt: feminine plural active participle of awrā (to strike fire); those whose hooves draw sparks from the rocks.
qadḥ: the act of striking the stone to draw fire from it; in the accusative as object.

versets.trad: and the ones who surge at dawn,
versets.commentaire: al-mughīrāt (المُغِيرَات), the ones who charge; ṣubḥ (صُبْح), the dawn. The hour of attack in ancient Arabia, when the enemy is still asleep. Another tradition sees in these verses the camels of the pilgrimage hurrying at the dawn of the day of sacrifice: the charge becomes a movement of devotion. Both readings meet in the same intuition — there is an hour when the soul must surge before the shadows return. And that hour, like the dawn, does not last.

mots.def: coordinating particle: and then.
mughīrāt: feminine plural active participle of aghāra (to charge, to raid).
ṣubḥ: dawn, early morning; in adverbial accusative: “at dawn.”

versets.commentaire: naqʿ (نَقْع): the raised dust. The course is so swift that the ground rises behind it, like a veil that marks the passage. A spiritual detail crosses the image: every great movement leaves its trace in the air. What the soul does, even in secret, lifts something around it.

mots.def: coordinating particle.
athāra: to raise, to send up (dust); in the feminine plural perfect.
preposition bi- + masc. sing. suffix pronoun: by that, in that place (the dawn or the fray).
naqʿ: dust raised by the feet of the beasts.

versets.trad: and break into the heart of the throng,
versets.commentaire: wasaṭna (وَسَطْنَ), they pushed into the middle; jamʿ (جَمْع), the gathering — and also, in pilgrimage usage, the name of Muzdalifa, where the pilgrims gather. The oath crests on this image of a center pierced. And the sūra, at the moment the fray is reached, pivots. The next verse no longer speaks of horses: it speaks of the heart of man.

mots.def: coordinating particle.
wasaṭa: to be in the middle of, to pierce to the center; in the feminine plural perfect.
preposition bi- + suffix pronoun.
jamʿ: gathering, host, fray; also the name of Muzdalifa during the pilgrimage.

versets.trad: truly, man is ungrateful to his Lord,
versets.commentaire: Here is the word in which everything concentrates: kanūd (كَنُود). Qushayrī gives three successive portraits of it, like three angles of light on one face. First: “He is the one who sees the trial that touches him, and does not see the favour that fills him.” The ungrateful is not a wicked man; he is a heart looking the wrong way. Then: “He is the one whose head rests upon the cushion of grace, but whose heart wanders in the field of heedlessness (ghafla, غَفْلَة).” The favour is right there, under his neck; the heart is elsewhere. Finally, more simply: “He is the one who forgets the favours and counts the misfortunes.” Tustarī goes further: the ungrateful is the one who has broken the Covenant (al-ʿahd, العَهْد), has parted from truthfulness, and has grown intimate with his passion. At that point, he says, God leaves him without hope of good or of saving fear. Four traits of the same word — and every one of them concerns us, in our lower hours.

mots.def: emphasis particle: truly, indeed.
insān: the human being, the generic man.
preposition li- + rabb + suffix pronoun: to his Lord.
affirmative particle.
kanūd: ungrateful; the word evokes also barren ground where nothing grows, and the one who forgets only the favours received.

versets.trad: and to that he himself can testify;
versets.commentaire: Two readings, and Qushayrī keeps both. He is man: he is the first witness of his ingratitude. Deep within, he knows — and it is this repressed knowing that makes ingratitude possible. Or else He is God: “And God, over this state of the servant, is witness,” writes Qushayrī. The two senses together close off every escape. Man knows himself ungrateful, and God sees man knowing himself ungrateful. There is no third party to call. The only move possible, within this vice, is to turn the face.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
inna + suffix pronoun: indeed, he.
preposition: on, about.
masc. distal demonstrative: that (referring to v. 6).
affirmative particle.
shahīd: witness; according to the commentators, either God or man himself bearing witness against himself.

versets.trad: but his love of riches is stronger.
versets.commentaire: The word Arabic uses here for “riches” is al-khayr (الخَيْر) — literally “the good.” The language thus says aloud what the heart, in secret, believes: that what one possesses is the good. Tustarī unmakes this belief by distinguishing three kinds of love that are mistaken for the good: love of the lower self (ḥubb al-nafs, حُبّ النَّفْس), love of the world (ḥubb al-dunyā, حُبّ الدُّنْيا), love of passion (ḥubb al-hawā, حُبّ الهَوى). And he names the true good, in three things likewise: to become independent of people through contentment, to recognise one's absolute neediness for God (iftiqār, افْتِقَار), and to fulfil His commandments. Iftiqār stands at the center: the true poor, who holds nothing in his hands, is rich of the One he awaits.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
inna + suffix pronoun.
ḥubb: love, attachment; preposition li- + noun in the genitive.
khayr: the good, what is good; here by extension, material goods, riches.
affirmative particle.
shadīd: intense, violent, firm; here, qualifies the excessive love.

versets.ar: أَفَلَا يَعْلَمُ إِذَا بُعْثِرَ مَا فِي الْقُبُورِ
versets.tr: a-fa-lā yaʿlamu idhā buʿthira mā fī l-qubūr
versets.trad: Does he not know that, on the day when what is in the graves shall be overturned
versets.commentaire: The verb buʿthira (بُعْثِرَ) does not say only “to resurrect”: it says to overturn, to empty out by tipping over, as one upends a vessel so it gives up its bottom. The graves are only half of what will be overturned. The other half — the more unsettling one — comes in the next verse.

mots.def: interrogative particle.
fa- + lā: not (negation in the interrogation).
ʿalima: to know.
temporal particle: when.
buʿthira: to be overturned, to be tipped out in full reversal; perfect passive with future value.
mots.gram: perfect passive verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: neuter relative pronoun.
preposition: in.
qubūr: plural of qabr, the grave.

versets.trad: and the contents of the breasts brought out,
versets.commentaire: ḥuṣṣila (حُصِّلَ): to be extracted, as one draws metal from the mine, or gathers in the harvest. Qushayrī: what was kept hidden in the hearts will be brought into the open, the good as much as the evil. The sūra thus opens a second reversal, graver than that of the graves: the overturning of inwardness. The great day, Qushayrī says elsewhere, is not only the Day of Judgment — it is, already, every hour when the heart consents to let itself be seen. The servant who keeps no secret from his Lord is quietly preparing that Day before it comes.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
ḥaṣṣala: to draw out, to gather, to bring out what was hidden; perfect passive with future value.
mots.gram: perfect passive verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: neuter relative pronoun.
preposition.
ṣadr: the breast, seat of the inmost heart; plural ṣudūr.

versets.trad: that Day, their Lord shall be fully informed concerning them.
versets.commentaire: khabīr (خَبِير) — one of the divine Names: He who knows from within, who knows the thing by the very experience He has of it. The verse teaches God nothing: He already knows. What changes that Day is that man, at last, will know that God knew. The sūra, opened on panting horses, closes on this silence: God, already, is informed of everything. And whoever knows it now has already received, before the hour, his share of light.

mots.def: emphasis particle.
rabb + plural suffix pronoun: their Lord.
preposition bi- + plural suffix pronoun: concerning them.
yawm + idh: that day (the Day of Judgment).
affirmative particle.
khabīr: He who knows by deep experience, who knows the depth of things; divine Name.
